Equity Research · Initiation of Coverage
Industrials — Electrical Equipment
2026-07-01
Initiation of Coverage

Atkore Inc.

NYSE: ATKR
InitiationElectrical ProductsSmall/Mid-cap · $2.4B

Executive Summary

Atkore is a scale manufacturer of electrical conduit and related "must-stock" building products whose earnings are set by the price-cost spread on commodity inputs — and that spread is at a cyclical trough. LTM Adjusted EBITDA of $321m is less than a quarter of the FY2022 peak because pandemic-era conduit pricing unwound; volumes never collapsed. The base case models a mid-cycle recovery — Adjusted EBITDA margin rebuilding from 12.4% (FY26, = guidance) to 15.0% by FY2030 (~$487m EBITDA) — and on that path the DCF spans $54.70–$73.59 (base $62.05) against a $72.43 price: the market is paying for the recovery, and little more (reverse-DCF: ~3.5% perpetual FCF growth or a ~6.9x exit, low end of realized history). Meanwhile the stock still trades at a 40%+ discount to core electrical peers on forward earnings with a 5.9% FCF yield. The verdict is Fair Value — the price sits at the top of the intrinsic band, offset by the relative discount; the swing factor is margin durability (reversion to a ~13% path is worth roughly −$13/sh). The technical setup is an unconfirmed repair, so the stance is wait for confirmation or a better level (~$65–68, inside the DCF range).

Financial Snapshot

($mm unless noted) LTM (Mar-26) FY2026E (model, guidance-calibrated)
Revenue 2,874 2,830
Adjusted EBITDA 321 351 (guided 340–360)
Adj. EBITDA margin 11.2% 12.4%
Adjusted diluted EPS ($) — ~5.30 (guided 5.05–5.55)
Free cash flow (CFO − capex) 145 191
Net debt 318 158
Net debt / Adj. EBITDA 1.0x 0.5x
Enterprise value (at $72.43) 2,764 —

Investment Thesis

  • The FY24–25 earnings collapse was a price event, not a volume event: ASP fell 12–17%/yr while volumes stayed positive in five of six quarters — the franchise (share, shelf, distribution) is intact; only the pricing premium is gone.
  • Pricing has just inflected: 2Q26 ASP contribution turned +1.5% — the first positive price quarter since FY2022 — with volume +4.6%, and distributor sell-through (Rexel, WESCO, Sonepar) has turned.
  • The demand mix is drifting toward data-center and electrification conduit (~44% of sales, growing high-single-digit), a secular tailwind inside a cyclical portfolio.
  • The balance sheet keeps every option open: ~1.0x net leverage, ~10x coverage, $442m cash, and the base case runs net debt to ~$0 by FY2030 while funding the dividend and buybacks (~$1.35bn repurchased FY22–25) — the trough is survivable without dilution or distress.
  • On the 15% mid-cycle base case the stock is worth $55–74 vs $72.43 — the recovery is in the price; upside from here needs margins beyond 15% (each point ≈ $32m of terminal EBITDA) or a re-rate toward the historical 8x exit (~$81 at the grid's 8.5x column), while reversion to a ~13% path caps value in the high-$40s.

Key Strengths

  • #1 or #2 US share across most product lines, defended through the downturn — must-stock breadth and national distribution are real barriers in a freight-intensive category.
  • Low-cost manufacturing know-how (in-line Flo-Coat galvanizing, Cellular Core extrusion) — a durable cost edge that is trade-secret, not patent-dependent.
  • Safety & Infrastructure provides ballast: EBITDA held in a $90–138m band through a cycle that cut Electrical EBITDA by ~75%.
  • Modest leverage and ample liquidity at the trough — a structural, not cyclical, advantage over levered building-products peers.

Key Risks

  • Pricing power is conditional: new PVC/steel capacity and structurally lean distributor inventories cap the price leg of the recovery unless tariffs hold — a plateau near ~13% margins (the prior conservative path) is worth roughly −$13/sh versus the base case.
  • Customer concentration: Sonepar >10% of sales, top-10 ~40% — volume held in the downturn because Atkore conceded price.
  • Litigation overhang: $136.5m PVC antitrust settlement booked with a third class and a securities action still open — further cost and a read-across to the quality of peak-era earnings.
  • Governance transition: CEO retiring with the search open, CFO under two years in seat, plus an active strategic review — elevated execution risk for the recovery window.

Competitive Positioning

Within US electrical raceway products Atkore is the breadth-and-cost leader — the widest must-stock catalogue, leading share per line, and process cost advantages — but it is structurally the most price-cyclical name in its peer set: nVent, Hubbell and Eaton own higher-margin, spec-driven electrification franchises, while Atkore's commoditized conduit carries the beta. It is advantaged versus the long tail of regional converters and disadvantaged versus the diversified electricals on margin quality — which is precisely how the market prices it.

Entry Verdict

Fair Value — wait. The base-case DCF ($62.05, range $55–74 on a 15% mid-cycle margin path) puts the $72.43 price at the top of the intrinsic band, while relative multiples sit at a 40%+ discount to core comps; the lenses offset, and the price already reflects the recovery the base case assumes. Technically the repair is unconfirmed (price just under a rising 100-day EMA, momentum conflicting, light volume). Preferred entry is $65–68 at the 200-day average — inside the DCF range — or on a confirmed reclaim of ~$72.9 on above-average volume.

1Company Overview↑ Contents

What this section answers: what kind of business Atkore is, how it makes money, and where the fragilities sit.

Key takeaways

  • A cyclical spread business at a trough, not a secular grower — Atkore converts steel, copper and PVC into #1/#2-share "must-stock" electrical products, and its margin is the price-cost spread: LTM Adjusted EBITDA of $321m is less than a quarter of the FY2022 peak because pricing, not volume, unwound.
  • The central debate is where mid-cycle earnings normalize. Volume held positive through the downturn while ASP fell 12–17%/yr; guidance (~$350m FY26 EBITDA) plus a first positive price quarter (2Q26 +1.5%) mark the floor forming — but the pricing leg of any recovery depends on tariffs and capacity, not the channel restock.
  • Defining risks are concentration and credibility — Sonepar >10% of sales (top-10 ~40%), an impaired-and-divested HDPE M&A round trip, a $136.5m antitrust settlement, and a CEO search alongside a sub-2-year CFO. Modest leverage (~1.0x) is the offsetting cushion.

Initiating-coverage business DD. All figures from Atkore SEC filings (10-K/10-Q/8-K) and the company website unless a third-party source is cited. Fiscal year ends September 30. LTM anchor = trailing twelve months ended March 27, 2026 (fiscal Q2 FY26), the latest reported period.

1.1Company Description↑

Atkore manufactures electrical conduit, cable, and metal building components sold mainly through electrical and industrial distributors to contractors building and renovating non-residential structures. It is a volume-and-scale manufacturer: it converts steel, copper, PVC resin and HDPE into standardized code-compliant products where the company holds a self-described #1 or #2 US position by net sales across a large share of its lines.

  • The economic engine is spread — the gap between average selling price and raw-material input cost — amplified by scale, in-line galvanizing know-how (Flo-Coat) and a national distribution footprint. That makes reported margins highly sensitive to product-price cycles, most visibly PVC conduit.
  • Two reporting segments: Electrical (~70% of revenue) and Safety & Infrastructure (~30%). Headquartered in Harvey, Illinois; ~5,400 employees; 38 manufacturing facilities across eight countries.
  • LTM Revenue $2,874m, LTM Adjusted EBITDA $321.0m, LTM Adjusted EBITDA margin 11.2% (margin calculated = Adjusted EBITDA / Revenue; LTM ended March 27, 2026). Both metrics are near cyclical lows after a pandemic-era price spike unwound.

Read-through: Atkore is a cyclical spread business at a down-cycle low, not a secular grower. LTM Adjusted EBITDA of $321m is less than one-quarter of the $1,341.8m earned in FY2022. The investment question is where mid-cycle earnings normalize, not whether the recent peak was sustainable — it was not.

1.2Product Offering↑

Atkore sells a broad catalogue of "must-stock" electrical and mechanical products, packaged under one master brand (rebranded from a house-of-brands to a "branded house" in 2020) with legacy sub-brands retained for recognition.

  • Electrical: metal electrical conduit and fittings, PVC (plastic) conduit and fittings, electrical cable and flexible conduit, and cable management systems — the physical raceways and protection for building wiring. Product/brand pages: Allied Tube & Conduit, AFC Cable Systems, Heritage Plastics, Calpipe.
  • Safety & Infrastructure: metal framing and strut (Unistrut, Power-Strut), mechanical tube, cable tray/management (Cope, US Tray), and perimeter security. Product page: Unistrut.
  • Innovation is incremental and installation-labor-focused — examples cited by the company include patented MC Glide Tuff armored cable and the 20-ft Kwik-Fit Steel Conduit (twice standard length, fewer connectors). Most products carry Building Information Modeling (BIM) files for designers.

1.3Key Reporting Segments↑

Atkore reports two segments, evaluated by management on net sales and Adjusted EBITDA. Basis: FY2025 (latest full fiscal year); % of profit uses segment Adjusted EBITDA before unallocated corporate expense.

SegmentSegment DescriptionSegment Financials

Outer ring = % of FY2025 revenue; inner ring = % of FY2025 segment Adjusted EBITDA (before −$53.3m unallocated corporate). Electrical's 75% EBITDA share exceeds its 70% revenue share — it remains the margin driver even at a cyclical low.

Read-through: The two segments now look almost equally profitable in dollars of margin per unit — Electrical's FY2025 EBITDA margin (16.5%) is only ~370bp above S&I (12.8%), versus a ~2,700bp gap in FY2022. Electrical's premium is a pricing artifact, not a structural moat; S&I is the ballast.

1.4Geographies↑

Atkore is overwhelmingly a US business, and that concentration has been stable through the cycle. The US accounted for ~88% of net sales in FY2025 (FY2024 88%, FY2023 ~90%), sold to customers located in the United States; the balance is Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the UK and Belgium.

  • Reported net sales by geography ($m): US 2,501 / International 349 (FY2025); US 2,818 / 384 (FY2024); US 3,150 / 369 (FY2023). International revenue is small and modestly declining in absolute terms.
  • Manufacturing mirrors sales: 38 plants / ~8.6m sq ft in eight countries, but US-concentrated, with the largest facility at the Harvey, IL headquarters.
  • Geographic risk is therefore a US non-residential-construction and industrial-demand story, not an FX or emerging-market story. The 2026 divestitures of the Belgium coatings businesses further shrink the international tail.

2Revenue Model↑ Contents

Revenue is transactional and volume-driven, not contracted or recurring: Atkore sells standardized products into a distribution channel at prevailing market prices, recognizing revenue on shipment. There is no meaningful backlog, subscription or take-or-pay structure to smooth results.

  • Channel: ~83% of FY2025 net sales were distribution-based (electrical, industrial and specialty distributors); OEM sales were ~11.5%.
  • Pricing: effectively spot/market-indexed to commodity and product-price cycles. Selling prices for PVC and metal conduit rose sharply in 2021–2022 and have normalized down since — the single largest driver of the revenue and margin swing (FY2025 Electrical net sales −15.1%, almost entirely price).
  • Revenue-model risk: no contractual protection against price deflation; volumes are exposed to construction cycles and weather. The offset is that "must-stock" distributor status and short order cycles give resilient share and fast inventory turns rather than revenue visibility.

2.1Customers / Sales & Marketing / Distribution↑

Atkore sells primarily to large electrical distributors, who resell to contractors; concentration is moderate and rising at the top. The top ten customers were ~40% of FY2025 net sales, and one distributor — Sonepar USA — exceeded 10% of sales in each of FY2023–FY2025.

  • Customers: global distributors (CED, Graybar, Rexel, Sonepar, Wesco), super-regional and independent distributors, buying groups (AD IESD, STAFDA), industrial distributors and big-box retailers; plus solar/alternative-energy OEMs.
  • Go-to-market: commissioned external sales agents plus internal sales teams; a hub-and-spoke logistics offering ("ReliaRoutes") and digital order tools differentiate on service/delivery rather than price.
  • Marketing: single-brand "Atkore" strategy with retained sub-brands; targets distributors, contractors, engineers and OEMs.
  • Channel dependence: ~83% of sales route through distribution, making distributor inventory behavior (stocking/destocking) a first-order driver of reported sell-in.

The health of the largest customer currently supports, rather than threatens, the concentration. Sonepar reported record FY2025 revenue of $37.9bn with USA sales +15% YoY and record Americas EBIT, and its growth verticals (data centers, grid, industrial automation, solar) overlap Atkore's cited FY2026 volume drivers (Sonepar FY2025 results, GlobeNewswire, 24 Mar 2026, accessed 2026-07-01; detail in ATKR_Sonepar_channel_note_2026-07-01.md).

  • Sonepar is private and reports annually; no intra-year print exists, so Rexel and WESCO quarterly results are the live sell-through proxies — Rexel Q1-2026 +3.4% same-day (first growth after 11 consecutive quarters of decline), WESCO Q1-2026 +12.3% organic.
  • Quarterly price/volume decomposition shows the FY2024–FY2025 "destock" was almost entirely a price event, not a volume event: Atkore ASP fell 13–17% YoY through FY2025 while volume stayed positive in 5 of 6 quarters — distributors kept buying units but stopped paying 2022 prices, and Atkore defended share via its stated dynamic-pricing strategy.

Watch item: Single-distributor dependence (Sonepar >10%) plus top-10 at ~40% remains a structural bargaining-power exposure — volume held through the downturn because Atkore conceded price, not because of customer loyalty at stable prices. The channel is also structurally leaning inventory (VMI, digital procurement), so a demand recovery reads as normalisation of sell-in to sell-through rather than a restock snapback.

2.2Demand Drivers↑

Demand is tied to US non-residential construction and electrification activity; management and third-party data point to the same end markets. Each driver below feeds product volumes (and, on the upside, pricing).

  • Non-residential construction & renovation (MR&R) — the largest driver; conduit/cable/framing content per project. Tied to the US non-residential construction cycle (see Industry Overview). Cyclical.
  • Data centers — a fast-growing electrical-content end market; cited by the company and by market researchers as a primary volume driver for conduit/cable. Secular. (Cable-conduit demand in North America is described as data-center-led — Grand View Research, accessed 2026-07-01.)
  • Electrification & renewables (solar) — mechanical tube for solar racking and conduit for utility-scale projects; solar exposure sits in Safety & Infrastructure and carries specific tax-credit accounting. Secular/regulatory.
  • Reshoring / infrastructure — semiconductor fabs, transportation and grid-hardening spend support conduit volumes. Policy-driven.

2.3Cost Drivers↑

The dominant cost is raw material: steel, copper, PVC resin and HDPE, converted in energy- and freight-intensive plants. Cost of sales was $2,174.3m in FY2025, ~76.3% of net sales, so gross margin is a thin, volatile residual (23.7% FY2025 vs 33.7% FY2024).

  • Variable (majority): steel, copper, PVC/HDPE resin, plus freight and energy. Margin is set by the price-cost spread; in FY2025 input costs rose while selling prices fell, compressing gross margin ~1,000bp.
  • Fixed/semi-fixed: plant labor (~20% unionized), depreciation (~$124.5m FY2025) and SG&A ($396.6m FY2025, ~13.9% of sales). Atkore is closing three plants in FY2026 to right-size fixed cost.
  • Atkore does not disclose a formal fixed/variable split; the ~76% COGS ratio and the price-cost commentary imply a highly variable, commodity-linked cost base (analyst characterization).

2.4AI Considerations↑

AI is a second-order factor for Atkore — neither a core disruption risk to demand nor a near-term margin lever, but a genuine indirect demand tailwind through data-center construction.

  • Opportunity (demand): AI-driven data-center buildout is one of the fastest-growing electrical-content end markets; every hyperscale facility consumes large volumes of conduit, cable and cable management — squarely Atkore's Electrical catalogue. This is the most material AI linkage.
  • Opportunity (operations): incremental — ERP/warehouse automation and digital ordering (BIM tools, order-tracking app) can trim working capital and service cost, but Atkore states IT has historically not been a differentiator.
  • Risk: low. Standardized, code-driven physical products are not substitutable by software; the threat is indirect (construction-cycle demand), not technological disruption.

2.5Research & Development↑

Atkore is not an R&D-intensive company and does not separately disclose an R&D expense line; product development ("faster/easier to install" SKUs like Kwik-Fit and MC Glide Tuff) is funded within SG&A and cost of sales.

  • No R&D / sales ratio is derivable from the filings (not disclosed). Innovation is described qualitatively (patents, BIM files, labor-saving designs) rather than quantified.
  • Versus peers, this is normal for the electrical-products/building-products category (e.g., nVent, Hubbell disclose modest R&D; commodity conduit peers disclose little). Treat Atkore as a low-R&D, process/scale competitor — advantage is manufacturing cost (Flo-Coat galvanizing) and distribution, not proprietary technology.

2.6Suppliers↑

Inputs are commodity metals and resins bought from large, established suppliers; Atkore describes sources as well-established and generally available, with named multi-supplier relationships that limit single-source risk.

  • Steel: Cleveland-Cliffs, Steel Dynamics, Nucor. Copper: AmRod, SDI LaFarga, Nexans. PVC resin: Westlake, Formosa, Oxy Vinyls. HDPE resin: LyondellBasell, Baystar.
  • Supplier concentration: multiple qualified suppliers per input — no single-source dependency disclosed. Supplier bargaining power is nonetheless high because inputs are exchange-priced commodities Atkore cannot control.
  • Supply-chain risks: commodity price volatility (the core margin risk), plus import competition and tariff/trade-policy exposure on both finished-goods imports and inputs; Nucor and Steel Dynamics are also competitors in Electrical, a notable supplier-competitor overlap.

2.7Employees↑

Atkore employed ~5,400 full-time-equivalent employees at September 30, 2025 (~6% temporary/contract), primarily in the US with ~15% international.

  • Unionization: ~20% of domestic and international employees are covered by collective bargaining agreements, all in the US or Canada. The Harvey, IL CBA with the United Steelworkers was ratified April 3, 2025 (near-term renewal risk at the flagship plant therefore low).
  • Management describes labor relations as "good"; the company is Great Place to Work-certified (five consecutive years). ~20% of FY2025 role fills were internal promotions.
  • Headcount is being trimmed via the three FY2026 plant closures and 2025–2026 divestitures (HDPE, Belgium), consistent with cost right-sizing.

2.8Key IP or Facilities↑

IP is supportive, not foundational: Atkore owns a portfolio of patents and trademarks but explicitly states its competitive position does not depend on any single patent or group of patents.

  • Proprietary process: in-line galvanizing (Allied Tube's Flo-Coat) and Cellular Core conduit co-extrusion — genuine low-cost manufacturing know-how, largely trade-secret rather than patent-dependent.
  • Facilities: 38 manufacturing plants, ~8.6m sq ft, eight countries; HQ and largest plant in Harvey, IL. Three plants being closed in FY2026 (footprint rationalization). Owned-vs-leased split not itemized here (see 10-K Item 2).
  • Brands (Allied Tube, Unistrut, AFC Cable, Heritage Plastics, Cope) carry channel recognition and are a real, if modest, intangible asset.

2.9Key Acquisitions and Divestitures↑

Atkore executed an aggressive debt-funded "buy-and-build" program in FY2021–FY2022 — then reversed much of it via impairments and divestitures in FY2025–FY2026, a value-destructive round trip concentrated in HDPE pipe/conduit. Most-recent-first:

  • Apr 30, 2026 — Divested Vergokan Galva NV and Vergo Coating SRL (Belgium surface-protection/powder-coatings). Price not disclosed. Rationale: focus on core electrical infrastructure; exit non-core international.
  • Apr 8, 2026 — Divested HDPE Pipe & Conduit business to Infra Pipes (5 US sites, telecom-focused). Atkore contributed the business, will fund ~$28m of cash, and retained a 10% equity stake — effectively paying to exit. Rationale: exit a structurally weak, impaired business.
  • FY2025 — Divested Northwest Polymers (recorded a $6.2m loss on sale); impaired HDPE assets by $194.5m and Mechanical-unit goodwill by $18.9m ($214.4m total FY2025 impairment).
  • FY2022 — Acquired six businesses for >$310m as part of a Nov-2021 plan to deploy >$1bn on M&A over 2–3 years: Sasco Tubes & Roll Forming and Four Star Industries (Dec 2021), Talon (May 2022), United Poly Systems (Jun 2022), Cascade Poly Pipe & Conduit and Northwest Polymers (Aug 2022), and Elite Polymer Solutions (~$91.6m, Nov 2022, HDPE conduit). (Plastics News; Canvas Business Model — Atkore history, accessed 2026-07-01.)
  • FY2021 — Acquired Queen City Plastics and FRE Composites Group (contributed to FY2021 net-sales growth).

Read-through: The HDPE thesis (built via United Poly, Cascade, Elite Polymer, Northwest Polymers in 2022) was impaired ~$194.5m and divested at negative net proceeds by 2026 — a direct hit to capital-allocation credibility and a caution on any renewed M&A ambition. Purchase multiples for the 2022 deals are not disclosed and cannot be reliably calculated (see data-gaps).

2.10Collateral Overview↑

Working-capital assets and PP&E are substantial and largely commodity-linked; balances swing with metal prices and construction seasonality. Latest actual balances (March 27, 2026):

  • Accounts receivable, net: $557.9m — up sharply from $447.0m at FY2025 year-end on seasonal Q2 build (spring construction ramp); allowance modest at ~$2.1m.
  • Inventories, net: $401.1m — down from $484.8m at year-end as the company de-stocked into lower prices; inventory value is exposed to commodity mark-downs.
  • Prepaid & other current assets: $89.8m.
  • PP&E, net: $534.7m — declining ($594.3m at FY2025 year-end) on divestitures, plant closures and capex running below depreciation.
  • Goodwill ($287.5m) and intangibles ($127.0m) remain after the FY2025 impairments but are not working-capital collateral. AR and inventory together (~$959m) are the primary liquid collateral, typical for an ABL-backed distributor-facing manufacturer.

2.11Management Overview↑

Atkore is in the middle of a leadership transition at the top, which coincides with a strategic-portfolio review — a governance situation worth close attention.

  • President & CEO — William (Bill) Waltz. ~12 years at Atkore, seven as CEO (CEO since 2018). Announced his decision to retire on August 5, 2025; will serve until a successor is named while the Board runs a formal CEO search. (Businesswire, accessed 2026-07-01.)
  • VP & CFO — John Deitzer. CFO since August 2024 (succeeded David Johnson). Joined Atkore in 2019 as VP Investor Relations, later VP Finance for the Electrical unit; ~19 years' experience. MBA (Carnegie Mellon, Tepper); BS Chemical Engineering (Case Western). (Businesswire — CFO transition, accessed 2026-07-01.)
  • Chairman of the Board — Michael Schrock (non-executive).
  • Background / litigation / controversy check: No material adverse findings identified against the named individuals personally from reliable public sources (searches of company filings and press, 2026-07-01). The material legal exposure is corporate, not individual — the PVC Pipe antitrust matter and a related securities class action (see Other Risks). "No material issues identified" at the individual level, on a conservative bar.

Watch item: CEO succession + CFO in role <2 years + an active review of strategic alternatives + a large litigation settlement is a heavy change agenda for one period. Execution/continuity risk is elevated until a permanent CEO is named.

2.12Other Risks / Comments↑

Company-specific risks beyond the industry cycle (industry-level risks are in the Industry Overview):

  • PVC antitrust & securities litigation (material). Atkore recognized a $136.5m settlement in Q2 FY2026 with two of three putative classes in In re PVC Pipe Antitrust Litigation; a third class remains, and a related securities class action alleges the prior pricing/earnings were inflated by the alleged conduct. Further settlement/defense cost and reputational risk remain open.
  • Capital-allocation track record. The 2022 HDPE build → 2025 impairment → 2026 divestiture sequence weighs on management credibility on M&A and capital deployment.
  • Customer concentration. Sonepar >10% of sales; top-10 ~40%.
  • Earnings quality / GAAP-vs-adjusted gap. FY2025 GAAP operating income was just $23.2m and net income was negative, versus $386.4m Adjusted EBITDA, on $214.4m impairments; Q2 FY26 showed a $124.1m net loss driven by the litigation settlement and held-for-sale losses. Adjusted metrics require scrutiny.
  • Leverage is modest (net debt ~$318m, ~1.0x LTM Adjusted EBITDA) — a genuine mitigant that gives the down-cycle balance-sheet room (capital-structure detail belongs to the financial section).

Open items and undisclosed fields are logged in ATKR_data_gaps.md; third-party citations are consolidated in step2_appendix.json (§5.6).

3Industry Overview↑ Contents

What this section answers: whether industry structure helps or hurts Atkore's margin and growth prospects.

Key takeaways

  • Structurally advantaged on cost and shelf, not on price — scale galvanizing, national distribution and must-stock breadth defend Atkore's share, but in a commoditized, oligopoly-at-the-top industry, capacity additions and distributor power cap pricing once shortage premiums unwind.
  • Demand mix is drifting the right way: base non-resi demand grows ~3–5% but data-center and electrification niches grow ~6–8%, and Atkore's conduit lines (~44% of sales) are the direct beneficiaries; commoditized cable (17%, declining) is the wrong-side exposure.
  • The destock is over but it was a price event — sell-through has turned (Rexel +3.4%, WESCO +12.3% in Q1-26) and sell-in has converged, yet a restock does not mechanically restore price: the industry margin question is the mid-cycle spread, which hinges on tariff protection and capacity discipline.

Initiating-coverage industry DD for Atkore Inc. (NYSE: ATKR). Company facts from Atkore filings; market-size, growth and competitor-scale figures are third-party and cited inline (access date 2026-07-01). Where sources disagree materially, the range is shown rather than a false-precision point estimate.

3.1Industry Definition, Size & Characteristics↑

The relevant industry is electrical raceway and building infrastructure products — the physical conduit, cable, fittings, metal framing and cable-management that route and protect electrical wiring in buildings and infrastructure. Atkore's core is the US electrical conduit and cable-conduit systems sub-segment (Electrical) plus metal framing / mechanical / cable-support products (Safety & Infrastructure); adjacent-but-out-of-scope are the wire/cable commodity itself, switchgear and electrical distribution equipment (Eaton/ABB/Schneider territory).

  • Market size — scope-dependent, cite with care. The narrow US/North America electrical conduit market is put at ~US$1.74bn (2024), growing to ~$2.24bn by 2032 (~3.2% CAGR) by Fortune Business Insights. The broader US cable-conduit systems market (including cable management/support) is sized far larger — roughly US$8.5–9.2bn (2026) rising to US$12.5–14.0bn by 2035, ~4.2–5.5% CAGR (Intellectual Market Insights). The order-of-magnitude gap reflects definitional scope; treat any single TAM as indicative, not precise.
  • Character: mature, cyclical, and commoditized-but-branded. Volumes track non-residential construction; product prices track steel/copper/PVC. It is consolidated at the top in the US (a few scale manufacturers per product line) yet fragmented in the long tail of regional players. Barriers are cost/scale and distribution access, not technology.
  • Scale/maturity markers: North America is the largest region (~31% of global cable-conduit revenue, 2025, per Grand View), and US demand is closely correlated with the ~US$1.1tn US non-residential construction sector and, increasingly, with data-center capital spending.

3.2Structural Trends & End-Market Demand↑

Underlying demand growth is low-to-mid single digit in the base market, but with a widening dispersion: legacy non-residential construction grows ~3–5% while electrification-linked niches (data centers, renewables) grow ~6–8%. The near-term reported picture is distorted by price normalization, which has masked positive volume with sharply negative pricing.

  • Historical vs projected CAGR: base electrical-conduit demand ~3–5%/yr through 2030–2032 (multiple third-party estimates, cited above); specialty non-metallic and shielded conduit for data centers/renewables ~6–8% (Grand View Research — cable conduit).
  • End markets (tied to Atkore's mix):
  • Non-residential construction & MR&R — the largest end market; cyclical, tied to construction starts and rates. Atkore's dominant exposure.
  • Data centers — secular; the standout volume driver, AI-capex-led; conduit/cable-management content per facility is high.
  • Renewables / solar — secular but policy-sensitive (tax credits); Atkore's mechanical tube for solar racking sits here (S&I).
  • Reshoring / infrastructure / grid hardening — policy-driven; semiconductor fabs, transportation, IIJA-funded grid work support conduit volumes.
  • Technology change is incremental: shift toward non-metallic (PVC/HDPE) and pre-fabricated/labor-saving raceway, digital design (BIM) and modular install — evolutionary, not disruptive to the product category.

3.2.1End-market mix by product area↑

Atkore's revenue spans five key product areas, each with distinct end-market drivers; the mix and the direction of volume/mix growth reveal where demand is accelerating (data centers, PVC conduit) versus challenged (electrical cable). Basis: FY2026 H1 net sales of $1,387m (Atkore Q2 FY2026 investor presentation).

  • The mix is tilting toward electrification/data-center-levered lines (plastic + metal conduit ≈ 44% of sales, both growing) and away from commoditized cable (17%, declining) — a favorable secular drift within an otherwise cyclical portfolio.

3.3Industry Structure & Value Chain↑

The value chain runs commodity inputs (steel, copper, PVC/HDPE resin) → conversion by scale manufacturers → electrical/industrial distributors → contractors → building owners. Atkore sits at the manufacturing node and captures margin on conversion and product-price spread, while distributors capture logistics/availability margin and control the customer relationship.

  • Concentration: the US electrical conduit/framing space is oligopolistic per product line — a handful of scale players (Atkore, Zekelman, Nucor/Republic, Southwire, nVent) hold leading shares, with Atkore claiming #1 or #2 US positions across many of its lines. Precise CR4/HHI is not publicly disclosed (see data-gaps).
  • Barriers to entry: capital intensity of galvanizing/extrusion lines, national distribution and freight networks, "must-stock" shelf position at the >12,000 US electrical-distributor branches, and code/UL certification. These favor incumbents; they do not protect against price competition once capacity exists.
  • Value-capture shift: in price-deflation periods, margin pools migrate from manufacturers back toward distributors and contractors as manufacturers give back the pricing they captured in 2021–2022. The structural power of the distribution channel (led by Sonepar, Wesco, Graybar, Rexel, CED) is a persistent constraint on manufacturer margins.

3.3.1Channel inventory cycle: sell-in vs sell-through↑

The 2023–25 destocking cycle is over, but the recovery signature is normalisation, not a restock. Quarterly decomposition of manufacturer sell-in against distributor sell-through (detail and exhibit in ATKR_Sonepar_channel_note_2026-07-01.md) shows the destock operated through price, not units.

  • Sell-through has turned: Rexel Q1-2026 +3.4% same-day — first growth after 11 consecutive quarters of decline, the cleanest destock-over marker; WESCO Q1-2026 +12.3% organic with record backlog; Sonepar FY2025 a record $37.9bn with USA +15% (Rexel Q1-2026, GlobeNewswire; WESCO Q1-2026, PRNewswire, accessed 2026-07-01).
  • Sell-in decomposition: Atkore ASP fell 13–17% YoY through FY2025 (disclosed price drag −$381.8m in FY2025) while volume stayed positive in 5 of 6 quarters; by CY Q1-2026 all series converge positive with ASP +1.5% — sell-in ≈ sell-through with the price drag gone.
  • A restock does not mechanically lift price. Price is set by capacity and competition, not channel inventory direction: the industry has added capacity (steel-conduit expansions, new PVC entrants, Prysmian–Encore cable scale), distributors are structurally leaning inventory (VMI, digital procurement, regional fulfilment), and manufacturers' dynamic price-defence means restock volume flows through at defended prices. The price leg of any recovery is conditional on tariff protection plus tight capacity, not automatic.
  • Tariff-hedge wildcard: distributor pull-forward ahead of 2026 tariffs (WESCO built inventory in 2025, already reversing) can make sell-in temporarily exceed sell-through — a short-cycle benefit to domestic manufacturers that later reverses.

3.4Competitive Landscape & Positioning↑

Competition is product-line-specific, ranging from large diversified industrials to regional conduit makers, and increasingly includes Atkore's own steel suppliers. Atkore competes on breadth, brand, service/delivery and manufacturing cost, not on price leadership or proprietary technology.

  • Electrical competitors: Zekelman Industries, Nucor Corporation, Southwire, Dura-Line, Prysmian, Mitsubishi — several (Nucor, Steel Dynamics) are also steel suppliers, an unusual supplier-competitor overlap.
  • Safety & Infrastructure competitors: Eaton, ABB, Hubbell, nVent Electric, Zekelman, Haydon — larger diversified electricals that out-scale Atkore in adjacent categories.
  • Positioning: Atkore's edge is a broad "one-stop" catalogue with leading US share in conduit/framing, strong distributor relationships and low-cost in-line galvanizing (Flo-Coat). Its disadvantage is a narrower, more commoditized, more price-cyclical portfolio than diversified peers (Eaton, Hubbell, nVent) whose electrification-levered franchises trade at premium multiples.
  • Market share: Atkore's own "#1 or #2" claims are the primary evidence; precise, independently-sourced US share by product is not reliably disclosed and should not be stated with false precision. Share appears defended (must-stock status) but pricing-driven revenue swings obscure underlying volume-share trends.

3.5Margin Dynamics: Bargaining Power & Key Constraints↑

Margins are set by the price-cost spread on commodity products sold through a concentrated distribution channel — the two forces that matter are supplier (input-commodity) power and customer (distributor) power, both structurally high, which is why "normal" margins sit well below the 2021–2022 peak.

  • Supplier power (high): steel, copper and resin are exchange-priced; Atkore cannot control input cost and, in FY2025, rising inputs met falling selling prices, compressing gross margin ~1,000bp. Multi-sourcing limits availability risk but not price risk.
  • Customer/channel power (high and rising): distribution is consolidated (Sonepar >10% of Atkore sales; top-10 ~40%). Distributors can de-stock rapidly in deflation, amplifying volume and price declines — a key mechanism behind the FY2024–FY2026 earnings reset.
  • The binding constraint is therefore pricing, not supply: Atkore is a price-taker on inputs and a price-follower on outputs once the pandemic-era shortage premium unwinds. The durable question for the industry (and the stock) is the mid-cycle spread — where conduit pricing settles above cost — rather than unit demand, which is modestly growing.
  • Near-term price read: the sell-in ASP inflection to +1.5% (CY Q1-2026) reflects deflation-lapping plus input-cost pass-through, not restored pricing power — PVC specifically still carries a guided ~$50m price headwind into FY2026, and new PVC/steel capacity caps the upside absent tariff support.

Open industry questions (CR4/HHI, independent US market-share by product, mid-cycle PVC-conduit price) are logged in ATKR_data_gaps.md; third-party citations are consolidated in step2_appendix.json (§5.6).

4Financial Overview↑ Contents

What this section answers: the historical trend, credit standing, and the conservative projection path.

Key takeaways

  • Equity lens: the P&L is bottoming, with a mid-cycle recovery modeled — three straight years of price-led revenue decline end with FY26 guidance of $340–360m Adjusted EBITDA (margin 12.4% vs the 34.3% FY22 peak); the base case recovers margin to 15.0% by FY2030 (EBITDA ~$487m), still barely a third of peak.
  • Credit lens: the down-cycle is comfortably survivable — net debt ~$318m (~1.0x LTM EBITDA), ~10x interest coverage, $442m cash and FCF-funded (not divestiture-funded) deleveraging; LTM FCF of $145m (45% conversion) is depressed by the one-off $136.5m litigation accrual and seasonal working capital, not structurally.
  • Capital allocation is shareholder-heavy but scarred — ~$1.35bn of buybacks shrank the share count from ~48m to ~34m and a dividend was initiated in FY24, while the FY22 HDPE acquisition spree was impaired and exited at negative proceeds — a caution against paying for renewed M&A ambition.

Financial DD for the initiating-coverage report. All figures from Atkore SEC filings (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K/EX-99.1) and the model built on them. $ in millions except per share; fiscal year ends September 30; LTM = trailing twelve months ended March 27, 2026 (fiscal 2Q26). Projections (FY2026E–FY2030E) are the analyst's base case (mid-cycle margin recovery to 15.0% Adjusted EBITDA margin by FY2030E).

4.1Snapshot↑

Financial summary

Atkore is a cyclical, spread-driven electrical-products manufacturer at a down-cycle trough. LTM revenue of ~$2,874m sits ~27% below the FY2022 peak, and LTM Adjusted EBITDA of $321.0m is less than a quarter of the $1,341.8m earned in FY2022 — the unwinding of a pandemic-era PVC/metal-conduit price spike. The balance sheet is the mitigant: net debt ~$318m, ~1.0x LTM Adjusted EBITDA, with double-digit interest coverage.

4.2Summary financials↑

($MM unless noted)FY21FY22FY23FY24FY25LTMFY26EFY27EFY28EFY29EFY30E

5Financial-trend assessment↑ Contents

Revenue & margin trajectory (equity lens)↑

The story is a boom-and-normalization, not secular decline in volume. Revenue rose from $2,928m (FY2021) to a $3,914m peak (FY2022), then fell three straight years to $2,850m (FY2025) as selling prices — not volumes — reset.

  • Pricing is the swing factor — the net-sales bridge makes it unambiguous. The average-selling-price contribution to group revenue growth ran +55.4% (FY2021) → +34.0% (FY2022) → −16.5% → −11.5% → −11.9% (FY2025), while volume stayed modestly positive throughout (−3% to +5%). The boom and bust are entirely price; underlying volumes never collapsed. (Operating-KPIs band, Rev & Segment tab.)
  • The quarterly bridge shows the inflection. ASP contribution ran −12.0% (1Q25) → −16.6% (2Q25) → −12.2% → −6.8% → −2.7% (1Q26) → +1.5% (2Q26) — the first positive price quarter since FY2022 — while volume was positive in five of the six quarters (+4.6% in 2Q26). FX (+1.2%) and divestitures (−1.8%) are now separately recorded per quarter; the full price / volume / FX / acquisitions decomposition (% and disclosed $mm) is keyed on the Rev & Segment Operating-KPIs block, each column footing to the reported net-sales change.
  • Gross margin compressed ~1,000bp, from 33.7% (FY2024) to 23.7% (FY2025), as input costs (steel, copper, PVC) rose while prices fell — the classic spread squeeze.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin fell from 34.3% (FY2022) to 13.6% (FY2025) and 11.2% LTM. The trajectory (peak → trough) is the single most important fact for valuation.

Segment quality (equity lens)↑

Electrical carries the cyclicality; Safety & Infrastructure is the ballast.

  • Electrical Adjusted EBITDA margin collapsed from 42.3% (FY2022) to 16.5% (FY2025) — the pricing gift fully reversed. It remains ~70% of revenue and ~75% of segment EBITDA.
  • Safety & Infrastructure held its margin in a 10.6–15.4% band throughout, ending FY2025 at 12.8% — structurally lower but far steadier.
  • The FY2025 gap between the two segments' margins narrowed to ~370bp (from ~2,700bp in FY2022): Electrical's premium was a pricing artifact, not a durable moat.

Cash flow & FCF quality (both lenses)↑

Cash generation remains healthy through the downturn but is now noisier.

  • FY2025 CFO $402.8m, capex $107.1m → FCF ~$295.7m (76.5% FCF/EBITDA conversion) — strong, aided by working-capital release as inventory normalized.
  • LTM FCF is depressed (~$145m, 45% conversion), dragged by a negative operating quarter (1Q26 CFO −$55.5m) reflecting the $136.5m PVC-antitrust settlement accrual, seasonal receivables build and tax payments. This is a timing/one-off distortion, not structural.
  • Capex fell sharply ($218.9m FY2023 → $107.1m FY2025) — genuine capital discipline into the downturn.

Balance sheet & leverage (credit lens)↑

Leverage is modest and coverage strong — the key reason the down-cycle is survivable without stress.

  • Net debt ~$318m = ~1.0x LTM Adjusted EBITDA (0.66x at FY2025 year-end; the rise reflects the seasonal 2Q working-capital build and the litigation settlement, not new borrowing). Total debt is essentially flat (~$760m: a term loan + $400m 4.25% notes due 2031).
  • Interest coverage ~10x (LTM Adjusted EBITDA / net interest) and fixed-charge cover comfortable even at trough earnings.
  • Liquidity is ample: ~$442m cash plus an undrawn ABL. Deleveraging has been funded by FCF, not divestiture proceeds; the 2026 HDPE/Belgium exits were portfolio, not liquidity, moves.

Returns & capital allocation (equity lens)↑

  • ROIC has de-rated with the cycle — from boom-era highs toward high-single/low-double digits (LTM Adjusted NOPAT ÷ invested capital ≈ 9%), and will track the margin recovery.
  • Capital returns have been aggressive historically — ~$1.35bn of buybacks over FY2022–FY2025 (share count fell from ~48m to ~34m) plus a dividend initiated in FY2024 ($1.32/yr). Buyback pace has slowed at the trough; a $0.33 quarterly dividend continues.
  • Capital-allocation caution: the FY2022 HDPE build (~$300m+ of acquisitions) was impaired ~$194.5m and divested by 2026 at negative net proceeds — a mark against M&A discipline.

5.1Projection narrative (base case)↑

The model assumes a mid-cycle margin recovery — the analyst-elected base case, revised from the original conservative case — with Adjusted EBITDA margin reaching 15.0% by FY2030E, still far below boom levels.

  • Revenue grows from ~$2,830m (FY2026E) to ~$3,246m (FY2030E): FY2026 roughly flat (organic volume offset by the HDPE/Belgium divestitures) then +3–4%/yr on data-center, electrification and non-residential demand.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin recovers to mid-cycle — 12.4% (FY2026E, = guidance) to 15.0% (FY2030E) — so Adjusted EBITDA rises from ~$351m to ~$487m, still well below FY2024's $772m and the $1,342m FY2022 peak. The pandemic pricing premium is treated as non-recurring; the recovery reflects spread normalization plus mix (data-center conduit) and fixed-cost leverage from the FY2026 plant closures.
  • Unlevered FCF builds from ~$192m (FY2026E, elevated by working-capital release) to ~$207m (FY2030E), taking net debt to ~$21m (~0.0x) by FY2030E while funding the dividend and moderate buybacks.
  • Circular-free build: projected interest = rate × opening debt; projected cash is the balance-sheet plug from the projected cash-flow ending cash. The projected balance sheet balances to $0.00 in every forward year.

5.2Latest guidance & calibration↑

Management's FY2026 outlook (maintained at 2Q26): Adjusted EBITDA $340–360m; Adjusted net income per diluted share $5.05–$5.55. The base case is calibrated to land inside both ranges (FY2026E Adjusted EBITDA ~$351m; Adjusted EPS ~$5.30). Management withholds forward GAAP reconciliations. The DCF hand-off (unlevered FCF, and a proposed terminal block — WACC 10.0%, Gordon growth g 2.25%, cross-checked to a ~7.5–8.0x through-cycle exit EV/EBITDA) is carried on the DCF Inputs tab for the valuation step.

Model QA: plumbing ERR=0; statement identities tie across all 17 periods; balance check = 0.00 including all five projection years; Summary QA PASS. Basis, sources, non-GAAP definitions and data flags are on the Notes tab and in step3_appendix.json.

6Valuation & Entry↑ Contents

What this section answers: what the stock is worth, and whether now is the time to enter.

Key takeaways

  • Fair Value: the base-case DCF (15% mid-cycle margin) says $54.70–$73.59 (base $62.05) versus $72.43 — the price sits at the top of the intrinsic band — while the stock trades at a 42–46% discount to core comps on NTM P/E and EV/EBITDA with a 5.9% FCF yield, ~150 bps above the 10-year Treasury.
  • The price is paying for the recovery the base case assumes: reverse-DCF implies ~3.5% perpetual FCF growth or a ~6.9x exit — low end of ATKR's realized history; the swing factor is margin durability — reversion to the prior ~13% conservative path is worth roughly −$13/sh.
  • Entry stance: wait — the technical regime is an unconfirmed repair (price just below a rising 100-day EMA, MACD contradicting, light volume); preferred entry $65–68 at the 200-day average (inside the DCF range), or on a confirmed reclaim of ~$72.9 on volume.

As of 2026-07-01 · Price $72.43 · All DCF figures from _dcf/ATKR_dcf_result.json (base case revised at Gate C: 15.0% FY2030E Adjusted EBITDA margin); all multiples computed from inputs.

6.1Overall Valuation Assessment↑

The base-case DCF values ATKR at $62.05/sh (FCFF) — ~14% below the $72.43 price — with a $54.70 (FCFE cross-check) to $73.59 (7.5x exit multiple) range, so the top of the engine range sits just above the market price. The base case now embeds a mid-cycle recovery (Adjusted EBITDA margin 12.4% → 15.0% by FY2030E, EBITDA ~$487m vs ~$351m guided for FY26); the chief intrinsic caveat is that this recovery is assumed, not yet proven — only one quarter of positive pricing (+1.5% in 2Q26) is on the tape.

  • The comps still read the opposite way: ATKR trades at a deep discount to peers on every lens — though on trough LTM EBITDA of $321m (vs $1,342m FY22 peak), which inflates the EV/EBITDA cheapness and makes NTM P/E the cleaner lens.
  • DCF-vs-comps tension: the intrinsic anchor leads on value — the reverse-DCF now implies ~3.5% perpetual FCF growth or a ~6.9x exit, the low end of ATKR's realized 7–8x range, so the market price is consistent with the revised base case plus a modest premium; the relative discount remains the market's compensation for commodity-spread cyclicality.

Premium / discount to peers - EV/LTM EBITDA: 8.6x vs Core-comp Median 15.9x (−46%); full Peer Median 18.1x (−52%) — on trough, Adjusted-basis EBITDA. - NTM P/E: 11.8x vs Core-comp Median 20.5x (−42%); Peer Median 21.1x (−44%) — on consensus forward EPS. - FCF yield: 5.9% vs Core-comp Median 2.8% (+310 bps); the picture is consistent — cheap on all three lenses — but versus a peer set of structurally higher-margin, more secular names.

FCF yield vs cost of capital: 5.9% sits ~150 bps above the US 10-year Treasury (4.44%, 2026-06-30, Alpha Vantage) — a positive cash-flow risk premium — but below the 9.9% WACC / 10.8% cost of equity, and LTM FCF of $145m is itself at a cyclical trough.

Upside factors - Margin recovery beyond the 15.0% FY2030 base path — each point of EBITDA margin is worth roughly $32m of terminal EBITDA; a 16–17% path or a re-rate toward the historical 8x exit lifts value well above the current price (the 8.5x grid column reads ~$81). - Balance-sheet optionality compounds: the base case takes net debt to ~$0 by FY2030 while funding the dividend and buybacks — sustained repurchases at a discount to intrinsic value add per-share value beyond the DCF. - The relative gap: at a 42–46% discount to core comps on forward earnings, evidence that mid-cycle margins are durable would close part of the multiple gap independently of the DCF.

Downside factors - The 15% margin path is the thesis, not a fact: if import competition and PVC capacity cap margins near the prior conservative path (~13%), fair value falls back to the high-$40s and the price implies ~25%+ downside. - Customer concentration: Sonepar >10% of sales, top-10 ~40% — volume held in the downturn because Atkore conceded price. - Peer-relative cheapness is partly a basis artifact (subject Adjusted vs peer reported EBITDA; trough LTM) — the discount may not close mechanically.

Valuation verdict: Fair Value — the price sits inside the upper half of the intrinsic range ($55–74), the reverse-DCF asks for assumptions in line with the base case rather than heroic ones, and the consistent relative discount plus above-RFR FCF yield offset the modest intrinsic downside; risk/reward is balanced, with the swing factor the durability of the margin recovery.

Valuation is Fair Value (base-case DCF of $62.05 against $72.43, with the 7.5x-exit bull case ($73.59) just above the price and a consistent discount to peers), and the technical setup is an unresolved early repair with conflicting momentum. No entry urgency: wait for the EMA(100) reclaim to confirm on volume, or for a pullback toward the 200-day average near $68 — which would also sit inside the DCF range.

6.2Relative Valuation↑

ATKR screens cheap against its approved peer set on every computed lens, with the discount widest on EV/EBITDA — where trough earnings and the adjusted-vs-reported basis both flatter the optics — and most credible on forward P/E and FCF yield.

CompanyPriceUS$Mkt capUS$MLTM RevUS$MLTM EBITDAUS$MEBITDA mgn%EV/EBITDAxNTM P/ExP/FCFxFCF yld%Div yld%

6.3Data caveats↑

  • Core comps (weight these — the direct/central reads): NVT, HUBB, MLI, WMS, ROCK. The full-peer Mean/Median is pulled upward by ETN; lean on the Core-comp rows.
  • MLI: net cash (negative net debt) lowers its EV.
  • EBITDA basis: subject is Adjusted (model, excludes the FY25 goodwill impairment and SBC/restructuring add-backs); peers are reported (AV) — the basis difference modestly flatters the subject's relative margin/multiple.
  • Trough denominator: ATKR LTM Adjusted EBITDA ($321m) is at a cyclical low ($1,342m FY22 peak; guided ~$350m FY26) — the EV/EBITDA discount overstates steady-state cheapness.
  • Subject LTM = step-4 model (Adjusted EBITDA, Mar-26); peers = AV COMPANY_OVERVIEW → Yahoo Finance fills; all USD. Two feeds only.

6.4Intrinsic Valuation↑

The dual-DCF engine values ATKR at an Enterprise Value of $2,425.5m, equity value of $2,107.2m and $62.05/sh on the primary FCFF-at-WACC path — ~14% below the current $72.43 — on the Gate-C-revised base case (mid-cycle margin recovery to 15.0% by FY2030E).

Case Per share (FCFF basis) Anchor
Bear $54.70 FCFE at cost of equity (10.8%)
Base $62.05 FCFF Gordon, g 2.25% @ WACC 9.94%
Bull $73.59 7.5x exit EV/EBITDA @ base WACC
  • WACC 9.94%: Ke 10.80% (Rf 4.3% + β 1.30 × ERP 5.0%), after-tax Kd 4.18%, 87/13 equity/debt weights.
  • Drivers: revenue recovers from a −0.7% FY26 dip to +3.2–3.7%/yr (FY2030E $3,245.9m); Adjusted EBITDA margin climbs 12.4% → 15.0% (EBIT margin 6.9% → 10.0% after D&A and add-backs); capex rises 4.0% → 4.5% of revenue; tax 24%.
  • Diagnostics: (1) FCFF-vs-FCFE reconciliation gap is $249.7m (11.85%) — above the ~5% comfort band, driven by actual leverage running below the 13% D/V target and an amortizing, non-releveraging debt schedule; FCFE is the conservative bound. (2) Terminal value is 71.4% of EV — elevated but normal for a recovery-path forecast. (3) The Gordon terminal implies an exit of 6.1x FY2030E Adj. EBITDA vs ATKR's realized ~7–8x through-cycle range — the terminal remains conservatively set even on the revised margin path.

6.5Football Field↑

The three valuation methods and the 52-week trading range, triangulated against the current price:

Method Low Mid High
DCF (FCFF) $54.70 $62.05 $73.59
EV/EBITDA Comps $95.23 $161.89 $286.59
NTM P/E Comps $58.82 $129.70 $173.88
52-Week Range (market ref.) $53.49 — $90.16
Current price $72.43

The methods still diverge, but less starkly than under the prior conservative case: the DCF band now reaches the price at its top (the 7.5x exit case), while both comps bands sit largely above it — the mechanical result of applying healthy-peer multiples to ATKR's trough EBITDA and depressed forward EPS (peer-reported vs subject-Adjusted EBITDA basis noted once here). The price sits at the junction of the DCF band's top and the comps bands' bottom, inside the 52-week range — the market is paying for the mid-cycle recovery and little more.

6.6Valuation Stress Test↑

Both grids stress the FCFF value per share around the revised base case (bold). The terminal parameter runs down the rows, WACC across the columns.

**Terminal growth (g) × WACC — Gordon**
g \ WACC8.94%9.44%9.94%10.44%10.94%
1.75%68.4163.2458.7054.6951.11
2.00%70.6165.1260.3256.1052.35
2.25%72.9667.1262.0557.6053.66
2.50%75.5069.2863.8959.1955.04
2.75%78.2471.5965.8660.8856.52
**Exit EV/EBITDA × WACC — exit-multiple terminal**
Exit x \ WACC8.94%9.44%9.94%10.44%10.94%
5.5x59.5958.2356.9155.6154.35

The Gordon grid brackets the $72.43 price only in its most favourable corner (WACC ~8.9%, g ≥2.25%); the exit-multiple grid crosses it at ~7.5x — the middle of ATKR's realized through-cycle range. Reverse DCF: the current price embeds ~3.5% perpetual FCF growth (vs the 2.25% assumption) or a ~6.9x FY2030E exit — assumptions in line with, not beyond, the revised base case. Terminal-value reliance is 71.4% of EV with the Gordon terminal implying 6.1x exit; the thesis now breaks on whether the 15% mid-cycle margin is achieved at all, rather than on discount-rate drift — a reversion to the prior ~13% path is worth roughly −$13/sh.

6.7Technical Analysis↑

Technical read as of 2026-07-01. ATKR is in an early, unconfirmed repair: price sits just below a rising 100-day EMA after a steep FY25 downtrend, but momentum signals conflict — the setup is unresolved and offers no timing edge yet.

  • Trend: price $72.43 is −0.6% below the EMA(100) (~$72.9), but the EMA slope has turned up (+1.5%) — a recovering regime, not a confirmed uptrend. Bollinger %B is −0.06: price has slipped marginally below the lower band on the latest pullback.
  • Momentum: RSI(20) at 43 — tentative, below the 50 line that would confirm the repair. MACD histogram −0.93 and contradicting — the recent bounce has rolled over.
  • Volatility / volume: the pullback is occurring on light volume (47% of the 20-day average) — selling pressure is not aggressive, but the recovery lacks sponsorship either.
  • Balance of signals: 1 supportive (rising EMA100) vs 2 conflicting (MACD, %B). A clean MACD improvement with RSI reclaiming 50 on above-average volume would tilt the read toward a starter buy.

Key levels

Level Price Basis
Resistance 2 $90.16 52-week high
Resistance 1 ~$77.6 50-day moving average / June swing high
Pivot ~$72.9 EMA(100), currently just overhead
Support 1 ~$68.0 200-day moving average
Support 2 $53.49 52-week low

6.7.1Entry Assessment (combined)↑

Valuation is Fair Value (base-case DCF $62.05 vs $72.43, with the 7.5x-exit case ($73.59) just above the price and a consistent discount to peers) and the technical setup is unresolved — wait: there is no valuation urgency and no timing edge; let the repair confirm or a better level develop before entering. A pullback to the $65–68 zone would also sit inside the DCF range.

Preferred entry zone: $65–$68 (200-day moving average ~$68 after a light-volume fade, or on a confirmed reclaim of the EMA(100) at ~$72.9)

Key levels: | Level | Price | Basis | |---|---|---| | Support 1 | $68.00 | 200-day moving average | | Support 2 | $53.49 | 52-week low | | Resistance 1 | $72.90 | EMA(100) pivot | | Resistance 2 | $77.60 | 50-day moving average |

Technical analysis presented as research illustration only.

7Appendix↑ Contents

7.1Financials — Basis of Preparation↑

Reporting currency USD; units $ millions; US GAAP; fiscal year ends September 30 (4-5-4 calendar); quarterly filer. LTM = trailing twelve months ended March 27, 2026 (fiscal 2Q26) = FY2025 + H1 FY2026 - H1 FY2025 (sum of quarters 3Q25+4Q25+1Q26+2Q26). Continuing-operations basis; no discontinued operations presented separately. Post-FY2025 divestitures (HDPE Pipe & Conduit and Belgium coatings, both April 2026) are reflected in projected revenue/segments, not restated into history. Headline non-GAAP = company-defined Adjusted EBITDA (and Adjusted diluted EPS); 'Adjusted operating income' is analyst-derived (Adj EBITDA - D&A) and used only for ROIC. Projection base = FY2025 (last actual full year); FY2026E-FY2030E are model estimates (the analyst-elected CONSERVATIVE case). Historicals hand-transcribed from filings and tied to printed subtotals; balance check = 0.00 every period incl. projections. Data flags: FY2021 year-end balance sheet not in the dataroom (column blank); interim cash-flow shown at aggregate granularity (annuals fully itemized). Operating KPIs: consolidated net-sales YoY change decomposed into price (ASP), volume, FX, acquisitions/(divestitures) and solar-credit/other, transcribed per quarter and per fiscal year from the earnings-release net-sales bridges and 10-K MD&A (both % of prior-period sales and disclosed $mm contributions); each period foots to the reported net-sales change.

7.2Key Assumptions & Methodology↑

7.2.1Financial Model Assumptions↑

Conservative case: shallow, structural-pressure recovery. FY2026 calibrated to management guidance (Adj EBITDA $340-360m; Adj EPS $5.05-5.55); out-years transition to history/structure-based drivers. Circular-free (interest on opening debt; cash = BS plug).

Assumption Value Basis Source Type
Electrical revenue growth -1.5% FY26, then +3.5-4.0%/yr FY26 organic volume ~+5% offset by pricing + HDPE/Belgium divestitures; then mid-cycle end-market growth assumed
Safety & Infrastructure revenue growth +1.0% FY26, then +2.5-3.0%/yr Steadier infra/solar demand assumed
Group Adjusted EBITDA margin 12.4% FY26 -> 15.0% FY30 FY26 = guided $350m midpoint (sourced); out-years mid-cycle recovery to 15.0% — analyst base case revised at Gate C (assumed) assumed
Normalized non-GAAP add-back $32-35m/yr SBC ~$25m + modest restructuring; no impairment/litigation projected assumed
D&A % of revenue 4.0-4.3% FY25 4.4%; eases as capex normalizes assumed
Capex % of revenue 4.0% -> 4.5% FY25 3.8% trough discipline; normalize to mid-cycle assumed
Working-capital days DSO 58 / DIO 76 / DPO 42 (flat) FY25 realized levels assumed
Tax rate 24% Normalized ETR (FY24 19.5%; up on mix/geography) assumed
Debt schedule / rate on opening debt ~$760m gross, 4.5%, $3.7m/yr mandatory amort Term loan + $400m 4.25% notes due 2031 (10-K debt note); no assumed refinancing sourced
Dividend / buyback Div $46m->$55m (+5%/yr); buyback $50m->$100m $1.32/sh dividend policy (sourced); buyback pace analyst estimate (assumed) sourced
DCF terminal block (hand-off to S4) WACC 10.0%; Gordon g 2.25%; exit EV/EBITDA ~7.5-8.0x cross-check Rf 4.3% + beta 1.30 x ERP 5.0% = Ke 10.8%; Kd 5.5% pre-tax; ~87/13 E/D; g <= long-run nominal GDP; exit multiple = ATKR through-cycle range assumed

7.2.2Comps & Relative-Valuation Methodology↑

Peers: NVT, HUBB, MLI, WMS, ROCK (core) + ETN (broader, large-cap directional read-across; excluded from Core-comp stats). Every multiple computed from inputs (EV = mkt cap + net debt; FCF yield = FCF / mkt cap; NTM P/E = price / consensus forward EPS) — no vendor multiples lifted. Sourcing: Alpha Vantage COMPANY_OVERVIEW first (7/7 tickers), Yahoo Finance filled price/net-debt/FCF/forward-EPS gaps. All USD, no FX. EBITDA basis: subject Adjusted (step-4 model, LTM Mar-26); peers reported — the basis difference modestly flatters the subject. Subject LTM EBITDA is at a cyclical trough, overstating the EV/EBITDA discount. Subject LTM row (Revenue 2,874 / Adj EBITDA 321 / net debt 318 / FCF 145 / dividends 44, $mm) transcribed from ATKR_financial_overview.md Summary (LTM Mar-26 column).

7.3DCF Assumptions, WACC Build & Output Detail↑

Base case revised at Gate C: Adjusted EBITDA margin recovering to 15.0% by FY2030E (from the original 13.2% conservative case); revenue path unchanged. Football-field Bear=FCFE cross-check $54.70, Bull=7.5x exit $73.59, both from the same engine run (no scenario re-runs). EV $2,425.5m − net debt bridge (debt $760.6m, cash $442.3m) = equity $2,107.2m = $62.05/sh FCFF on 33.96m diluted shares; FCFE cross-check $54.70 (reconciliation gap 11.85%, flagged: actual leverage below the 13% D/V target, amortizing debt schedule). Terminal: Gordon g 2.25%; TV 71.4% of EV; implied exit 6.1x FY2030E Adj EBITDA vs realized ~7-8x through-cycle. Reverse DCF at $72.43: ~3.5% implied perpetual FCF growth or ~6.9x implied exit — in line with the base case. NWC ties to the S3 model: year-0 anchor = actual FY25 balance-sheet NWC $690.6m rolled on the model's days path (DSO 58 / DIO 76 / DPO 42), so the FY26 $36.3m working-capital release is captured in full and unlevered FCF matches the model's DCF Inputs tab. Both sensitivity grids (WACC x g, WACC x exit EV/EBITDA) emitted by the engine.

Component Value
Cost of equity 10.80% (Rf 4.3% + beta 1.30 x ERP 5.0%)
After-tax cost of debt 4.18% (5.5% pre-tax, 24% tax)
WACC 9.94% (87/13 E/D)
Terminal growth 2.25% Gordon (exit cross-check 7.5x)
Revenue path FY26-30 -0.7% then +3.2% to +3.7%/yr; FY2030E $3,245.9m
EBIT margin path 6.9% -> 10.0% (Adj EBITDA margin 12.4% -> 15.0%)
Tax rate 24% normalized
Debt schedule $760.6m @ 4.5%, $3.7m/yr amortization, no refinancing

7.4Peer Set Construction↑

Ticker Name Tier Rationale Basis Notes
NVT nVent Electric plc core Named S&I competitor; electrical connection/protection, electrification & data-center demand reported EBITDA; premium multiple
HUBB Hubbell Incorporated core Named competitor in electrical/utility products; distribution-channel read-across reported EBITDA; larger, richer margins
MLI Mueller Industries core Best commodity price-cost-spread read-across (copper products) reported EBITDA; net cash lowers EV
WMS Advanced Drainage Systems core Plastic extrusion through distribution into non-resi/infrastructure; resin-cost cyclicality reported EBITDA; Mar FYE
ROCK Gibraltar Industries core Comparable-size US building/infrastructure products manufacturer reported EBITDA; different mix
ETN Eaton Corporation plc broader Named competitor; electrification benchmark — directional only, far larger/diversified reported EBITDA; excluded from core stats

7.5Data Gaps & Open Items↑

Item Why It Matters Where Flagged
Normalized / mid-cycle Adjusted EBITDA (LTM $321m vs FY22 peak $1,342m) Core valuation anchor — where earnings settle post price-normalization Company Overview — Company Description / Other Risks
CEO successor identity & timing Governance & continuity risk during strategic review Company Overview — Management Overview
Remaining PVC antitrust class + related securities class action Open, potentially material legal cost after $136.5m settlement Company Overview — Other Risks
Scope/outcome of 'strategic alternatives' review Could mean further divestitures or whole-company sale Company Overview — Company Description
Fixed vs variable cost split Determines margin sensitivity / operating leverage in a downturn Company Overview — Cost Drivers
Customer concentration detail beyond top-10 (~40%) / Sonepar (>10%); Sonepar health triangulated via FY2025 + Rexel/WESCO proxies Revenue durability and channel bargaining power Company Overview — Customers
Sonepar intra-year visibility (private, annual calendar reporting; no H1-2026 print until ~Mar 2027) Largest customer's health only observable via Rexel/WESCO sell-through proxies between prints Company Overview — Customers
Channel inventory days / restock magnitude (no published distributor inventory series for conduit) Determines whether recovery is normalisation or a restock with pull-forward risk Industry Overview — Channel inventory cycle
Purchase price/multiple for FY2021–FY2022 acquisitions Capital-allocation quality; only Elite Polymer (~$91.6m) sourced Company Overview — Key Acquisitions and Divestitures
Earnings-call transcripts (10 quarters) unavailable this run Management-commentary color absent (Alpha Vantage daily cap) Dataroom / run note
Mid-cycle PVC-conduit price benchmark Single largest swing factor for Atkore margins Industry Overview — Margin Dynamics
Independent US market share by product; CR4/HHI Sizes the competitive moat vs #1/#2 self-claim Industry Overview — Competitive Landscape / Industry Structure
Quantified revenue-by-end-market split (data center / solar / non-resi) Sizes secular vs cyclical demand mix Industry Overview — Structural Trends & End-Market Demand

8Disclosures↑ Contents

Analyst certification. The views expressed in this report accurately reflect the analyst's personal views about the subject security and issuer as of the date of this report. No part of the analyst's compensation was, is, or will be directly or indirectly related to the specific views expressed herein.

Rating definitions (entry scale — no price target is published). - Attractive — material intrinsic upside to base-case fair value and/or a discount to the core-comp median with a free-cash-flow yield above the risk-free rate; risk/reward skews favourably. - Fair Value — intrinsic value roughly in line with the current price and relative multiples broadly in line with peers; no clear edge either way. - Expensive — intrinsic downside and/or a premium to peers with a sub-risk-free FCF yield that growth or quality does not justify; risk/reward skews unfavourably.

Important disclosures. This report is for informational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice, an offer, or a solicitation to buy or sell any security. Figures are drawn from company filings, the analyst's financial model, and cited third-party sources as of the dates stated; estimates and projections reflect analyst assumptions that may prove incorrect. Technical analysis is presented as research illustration only. The analyst does not hold a position in the subject security. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Disclaimer. Information herein is believed reliable but is not guaranteed as to accuracy or completeness. Opinions and estimates are subject to change without notice. Recipients should conduct their own independent analysis before making any investment decision.

Sources & notes

Initiating-coverage research compiled from the company's filings, the data room and the analysis sections. Figures are as stated in the underlying sections. Not investment advice.
